eviction.court_process_summary_rule: {"statutes":"Md. Code, Real Property §§ 8-401, 8-402, 8-402.1","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"Notice to pay or quit varies by county; typically 5-day notice to pay or vacate under § 8-401","holdover_tenant":"1-month notice to quit for month-to-month (RC 8-402.1); 1-week notice for week-to-week tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice to cure for material breach (RC 8-402)","just_cause_required":"HB 18 (2025) requires just cause for eviction starting January 2026 in most counties"},"full_text":"Md. Real Property Code § 8-401. Failure to pay rent. (a) If rent is due and unpaid, the landlord may terminate the tenancy and recover possession of the premises in the same manner as if the tenancy was for a definite term but was created orally or in writing. (b) The landlord may give the tenant written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises within the time provided in the lease, or if no time is provided, within a reasonable time. (c) If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may bring an action for possession and rent.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-402. Breach of lease covenant. If the tenant breaches a covenant or condition of the lease, the landlord may give the tenant written notice specifying the breach and stating that the lease will terminate upon a date not less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied. If the breach is remediable and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within the notice period, the lease does not terminate.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-402.1. Holding over after expiration of term. If the tenant holds over after the expiration of the lease term, the landlord may bring an action for possession. For month-to-month tenancies, at least one month's notice to quit is required. For week-to-week tenancies, at least one week's notice to quit is required."}

sources.title: Maryland Code, Real Property § 8-203 (Security deposits)
extractedQuotes: A landlord may not impose a security deposit in excess of 1 month’s rent.
If a landlord ... meets the conditions for the exception, the landlord may impose a security deposit not in excess of 2 months’ rent.

sources.title:
{"statutes":"Md. Code, Real Property § 8-203","full_text":"Md. Real Property Code § 8-203. Security deposit.
(b) Maximum amount. A landlord may not charge or collect a security deposit in an aggregate amount that exceeds the equivalent of 2 months' rent. However, a landlord may collect, at the time of the initial rental period, a security deposit in an amount not exceeding the equivalent of 3 months' rent if the landlord informs the tenant, in writing, that the tenant is entitled to pay the security deposit in 2 monthly installments.
(e)(1) Return deadline. Within 45 days after the termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions, together with an itemized accounting of the deductions. (2) If the landlord makes a deduction, the landlord shall deliver to the tenant by first-class mail addressed to the tenant's last known address a written itemized list of the deductions together with a statement of the basis for and amount of any deductions and a refund of the security deposit. (3) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 45 days, the tenant may bring an action for damages for failure to return the security deposit.
(g)(1) Interest. A landlord shall pay interest on any security deposit held in a fiduciary capacity for a tenant at a rate equal to the greater of: (i) The rate paid on standard passbook savings accounts as set by the Federal Reserve; or (ii) One percent. (2) The interest shall be paid to the tenant within 30 days after the anniversary date of the tenancy and within 30 days after termination of the tenancy. (HB 269 of 2025 made changes to interest payment requirements.)"}
extractedQuotes: Maximum amount: Generally capped at 1 month's rent; limited exceptions can allow up to 2 months (confirm the exception criteria before charging more).

(3) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 45 days, the tenant may bring an action for damages for failure to return the security deposit.
(g)(1) Interest. (2) The interest shall be paid to the tenant within 30 days after the anniversary date of the tenancy and within 30 days after termination of the tenancy. (HB 269 of 2025 made changes to interest payment requirements.)"}

sources.title: {"statutes":"Md. Code, Real Property §§ 8-401, 8-402, 8-402.1","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"Notice to pay or quit varies by county; typically 5-day notice to pay or vacate under § 8-401","holdover_tenant":"1-month notice to quit for month-to-month (RC 8-402.1); 1-week notice for week-to-week tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice to cure for material breach (RC 8-402)","just_cause_required":"HB 18 (2025) requires just cause for eviction starting January 2026 in most counties"},"full_text":"Md. Real Property Code § 8-401. Failure to pay rent. (a) If rent is due and unpaid, the landlord may terminate the tenancy and recover possession of the premises in the same manner as if the tenancy was for a definite term but was created orally or in writing. (b) The landlord may give the tenant written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises within the time provided in the lease, or if no time is provided, within a reasonable time. (c) If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may bring an action for possession and rent.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-402. Breach of lease covenant. If the tenant breaches a covenant or condition of the lease, the landlord may give the tenant written notice specifying the breach and stating that the lease will terminate upon a date not less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied. If the breach is remediable and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within the notice period, the lease does not terminate.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-402.1. Holding over after expiration of term. If the tenant holds over after the expiration of the lease term, the landlord may bring an action for possession. For month-to-month tenancies, at least one month's notice to quit is required. For week-to-week tenancies, at least one week's notice to quit is required."}

(a) If rent is due and unpaid, the landlord may terminate the tenancy and recover possession of the premises in the same manner as if the tenancy was for a definite term but was created orally or in writing. (b) The landlord may give the tenant written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises within the time provided in the lease, or if no time is provided, within a reasonable time. (c) If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may bring an action for possession and rent.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-402. Breach of lease covenant. If the tenant breaches a covenant or condition of the lease, the landlord may give the tenant written notice specifying the breach and stating that the lease will terminate upon a date not less than 30 days after receipt of the notice if the breach is not remedied. If the breach is remediable and the tenant adequately remedies the breach within the notice period, the lease does not terminate.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-402.1. Holding over after expiration of term. If the tenant holds over after the expiration of the lease term, the landlord may bring an action for possession. For month-to-month tenancies, at least one month's notice to quit is required. For week-to-week tenancies, at least one week's notice to quit is required."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"Md. Code, Real Property § 8-208 (implied); common law","full_text":"Maryland does not have a comprehensive statutory provision governing landlord entry. Under common law, a landlord may enter a rental premises only: (1) At reasonable times; (2) Upon reasonable notice (typically 24 hours is considered reasonable); (3) For legitimate purposes such as repairs, inspections, or showing the property to prospective tenants or buyers; (4) In emergencies without notice. The tenant may recover damages for unlawful entry or harassment. There is no specific statutory notice period, but 24-hour advance notice is generally accepted as reasonable practice."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"Md. Code, Real Property §§ 8-211, 8-207","full_text":"Md. Real Property Code § 8-211. Landlord's failure to comply with lease; rent escrow. (a) If the landlord fails to comply with the landlord's obligations under the lease, the tenant may: (1) Bring an action for damages; (2) Withhold rent and deposit rent into court if the premises are not fit and habitable; (3) Terminate the lease if the landlord's noncompliance is material. (b) If the tenant deposits rent into court, the tenant shall continue to deposit rent as it becomes due throughout the court proceedings. (c) The tenant may recover damages for the landlord's failure to maintain the premises in a fit and habitable condition.\nMd. Real Property Code § 8-207. Mitigation of damages. (a) The landlord shall make reasonable efforts to mitigate damages caused by the tenant's breach of the lease. (b) The landlord shall mitigate damages by attempting to rent the premises to another tenant at a reasonable rent."}
